For the reasons listed above, the former channel is assumed to follow the former pattern unless VM_SEQ_READ or VM_RAND_READ is present, and the latter channel is assumed to follow the latter pattern unless outlying refaults have been observed. Workflow overview: Evictable pages are divided into multiple generations for each lruvec. The youngest generation number is stored in lrugen->max_seq for both anon and file types as they are aged on an equal footing. The oldest generation numbers are stored in lrugen->min_seq[] separately for anon and file types as clean file pages can be evicted regardless of swap constraints. These three variables are monotonically increasing. Generation numbers are truncated into order_base_2(MAX_NR_GENS+1) bits in order to fit into the gen counter in folio->flags. Each truncated generation number is an index to lrugen->folios[]. The sliding window technique is used to track at least MIN_NR_GENS and at most MAX_NR_GENS generations. The gen counter stores a value within [1, MAX_NR_GENS] while a page is on one of lrugen->folios[]; otherwise it stores zero. Each generation is divided into multiple tiers. A page accessed N times through file descriptors is in tier order_base_2(N). Unlike generations, tiers do not have dedicated lrugen->folios[]. In contrast to moving across generations, which requires the LRU lock, moving across tiers only involves atomic operations on folio->flags and therefore has a negligible cost. A feedback loop modeled after the PID controller monitors refaults over all the tiers from anon and file types and decides which tiers from which types to evict or protect. The desired effect is to balance refault percentages between anon and file types proportional to the swappiness level. There are two conceptually independent procedures: the aging and the eviction. They form a closed-loop system, i.e., the page reclaim. Aging: The aging produces young generations. Given an lruvec, it increments max_seq when max_seq-min_seq+1 approaches MIN_NR_GENS. The aging promotes hot pages to the youngest generation when it finds them accessed through page tables; the demotion of cold pages happens consequently when it increments max_seq. The aging uses page table walks and rmap walks to find young PTEs. For the former, it iterates lruvec_memcg()->mm_list and calls walk_page_range() with each mm_struct on this list to scan PTEs, and after each iteration, it increments max_seq. For the latter, when the eviction walks the rmap and finds a young PTE, the aging scans the adjacent PTEs. For both, on finding a young PTE, the aging clears the accessed bit and updates the gen counter of the page mapped by this PTE to (max_seq%MAX_NR_GENS)+1. Eviction: The eviction consumes old generations. Given an lruvec, it increments min_seq when lrugen->folios[] indexed by min_seq%MAX_NR_GENS becomes empty. To select a type and a tier to evict from, it first compares min_seq[] to select the older type. If both types are equally old, it selects the one whose first tier has a lower refault percentage. The first tier contains single-use unmapped clean pages, which are the best bet. The eviction sorts a page according to its gen counter if the aging has found this page accessed through page tables and updated its gen counter. It also moves a page to the next generation, i.e., min_seq+1, if this page was accessed multiple times through file descriptors and the feedback loop has detected outlying refaults from the tier this page is in. To this end, the feedback loop uses the first tier as the baseline, for the reason stated earlier. Working set protection: Each generation is timestamped at birth. If lru_gen_min_ttl is set, an lruvec is protected from the eviction when its oldest generation was born within lru_gen_min_ttl milliseconds. In other words, it prevents the working set of lru_gen_min_ttl milliseconds from getting evicted. The OOM killer is triggered if this working set cannot be kept in memory. This time-based approach has the following advantages: It is easier to configure because it is agnostic to applications and memory sizes; It is more reliable because it is directly wired to the OOM killer. mm_struct list: An mm_struct list is maintained for each memcg, and an mm_struct follows its owner task to the new memcg when this task is migrated. A page table walker iterates lruvec_memcg()->mm_list and calls walk_page_range() with each mm_struct on this list to scan PTEs. When multiple page table walkers iterate the same list, each of them gets a unique mm_struct, and therefore they can run in parallel. Page table walkers ignore any misplaced pages, e.g., if an mm_struct was migrated, pages left in the previous memcg will be ignored when the current memcg is under reclaim. Similarly, page table walkers will ignore pages from nodes other than the one under reclaim. This infrastructure also tracks the usage of mm_struct between context switches so that page table walkers can skip processes that have been sleeping since the last iteration. Rmap/PT walk feedback: Searching the rmap for PTEs mapping each page on an LRU list (to test and clear the accessed bit) can be expensive because pages from different VMAs (PA space) are not cache friendly to the rmap (VA space). For workloads mostly using mapped pages, searching the rmap can incur the highest CPU cost in the reclaim path. lru_gen_look_around() exploits spatial locality to reduce the trips into the rmap. It scans the adjacent PTEs of a young PTE and promotes hot pages. If the scan was done cacheline efficiently, it adds the PMD entry pointing to the PTE table to the Bloom filter. This forms a feedback loop between the eviction and the aging. Bloom filters: Bloom filters are a space and memory efficient data structure for set membership test, i.e., test if an element is not in the set or may be in the set. In the eviction path, specifically, in lru_gen_look_around(), if a PMD has a sufficient number of hot pages, its address is placed in the filter. In the aging path, set membership means that the PTE range will be scanned for young pages. Note that Bloom filters are probabilistic on set membership. If a test is false positive, the cost is an additional scan of a range of PTEs, which may yield hot pages anyway. Parameters of the filter itself can control the false positive rate in the limit. PID controller: A feedback loop modeled after the Proportional-Integral-Derivative (PID) controller monitors refaults over anon and file types and decides which type to evict when both types are available from the same generation. The PID controller uses generations rather than the wall clock as the time domain because a CPU can scan pages at different rates under varying memory pressure. It calculates a moving average for each new generation to avoid being permanently locked in a suboptimal state. Memcg LRU: An memcg LRU is a per-node LRU of memcgs. It is also an LRU of LRUs, since each node and memcg combination has an LRU of folios (see mem_cgroup_lruvec()). Its goal is to improve the scalability of global reclaim, which is critical to system-wide memory overcommit in data centers. Note that memcg LRU only applies to global reclaim. The basic structure of an memcg LRU can be understood by an analogy to the active/inactive LRU (of folios): It has the young and the old (generations), i.e., the counterparts to the active and the inactive; The increment of max_seq triggers promotion, i.e., the counterpart to activation; Other events trigger similar operations, e.g., offlining an memcg triggers demotion, i.e., the counterpart to deactivation. In terms of global reclaim, it has two distinct features: Sharding, which allows each thread to start at a random memcg (in the old generation) and improves parallelism; Eventual fairness, which allows direct reclaim to bail out at will and reduces latency without affecting fairness over some time. In terms of traversing memcgs during global reclaim, it improves the best-case complexity from O(n) to O(1) and does not affect the worst-case complexity O(n). Therefore, on average, it has a sublinear complexity. Summary: The multi-gen LRU (of folios) can be disassembled into the following parts: Generations; Rmap walks; Page table walks via mm_struct list; Bloom filters for rmap/PT walk feedback; PID controller for refault feedback. The aging and the eviction form a producer-consumer model; specifically, the latter drives the former by the sliding window over generations. Within the aging, rmap walks drive page table walks by inserting hot densely populated page tables to the Bloom filters. Within the eviction, the PID controller uses refaults as the feedback to select types to evict and tiers to protect.

title: How to troubleshoot Out of memory (OOM) killer in RHEL
raw_text: How to troubleshoot Out of memory (OOM) killer in Red Hat Enterprise Linux: Solution Verified - Updated October 28 2024 at 6:31 PM. Issue: The Out of memory (OOM) killer daemon is killing active processes. Services on Red Hat OpenStack Platform nodes are randomly dying. Memory usage seems to be high on Red Hat OpenStack Platform nodes. Available memory on Red Hat OpenStack Platform nodes seems to be low. There are "Out of memory: Kill process 43805 (keystone-all) score 249 or sacrifice child" noticed in the logs: [Wed Aug 31 16:36:42 2016] Out of memory: Kill process 43805 (keystone-all) score 249 or sacrifice child; [Wed Aug 31 16:36:42 2016] Killed process 43805 (keystone-all) total-vm:4446352kB, anon-rss:4053140kB, file-rss:68kB; [Wed Aug 31 16:56:25 2016] keystone-all invoked oom-killer: gfp_mask=0x280da, order=0, oom_score_adj=0; [Wed Aug 31 16:56:25 2016] keystone-all cpuset=/ mems_allowed=0; [Wed Aug 31 16:56:25 2016] CPU: 2 PID: 88196 Comm: keystone-all Not tainted 3.10.0-327.13.1.el7.x86_64 #1.

title: How does the OOM-Killer select a task to kill in RHEL
raw_text: Issue: How does the OOM-Killer select a task to kill? Understanding the OOM scoring mechanism. Environment: Red Hat Enterprise Linux 5, 6, 7, 8, 9. The OOM killer calculates an oom_score for each process. The score is based on the process's memory usage relative to total system memory. Processes can influence their score via /proc/PID/oom_score_adj ranging from -1000 to 1000. A value of -1000 makes the process immune to OOM killing. The kernel function oom_badness() computes the score: it sums the process's RSS, page table pages, and swap entries, then adjusts by oom_score_adj. The process with the highest score is selected as the kill target. Child processes sharing the same mm_struct are killed together. Resolution requires Red Hat subscription for full access.

title: How to disable or change the OOM Killer setting in RHEL
raw_text: Issue: How to disable or configure the OOM Killer in Red Hat Enterprise Linux. Environment: Red Hat Enterprise Linux 5, 6, 7, 8, 9. Options to control OOM behavior: 1) Per-process protection: echo -1000 > /proc/PID/oom_score_adj to exempt specific process. For systemd services add OOMScoreAdjust=-1000 in [Service] section. 2) System-wide disable via overcommit: set vm.overcommit_memory=2 in /etc/sysctl.conf to prevent memory overcommit, making OOM events unlikely. Set vm.overcommit_ratio to percentage of RAM available for overcommit (default 50). 3) Panic instead of kill: set vm.panic_on_oom=1 to reboot instead of killing processes, useful for HA clusters with watchdog. 4) Kill allocating task: set vm.oom_kill_allocating_task=1 to kill the process that triggered OOM rather than scanning all processes. Apply changes: sysctl -p or sysctl --system. Resolution requires Red Hat subscription for full access.

title: How to interpret OOM Killer messages in RHEL system logs
raw_text: Issue: Understanding OOM Killer log messages in Red Hat Enterprise Linux system logs. How to read and interpret dmesg or /var/log/messages OOM entries. Environment: Red Hat Enterprise Linux 6, 7, 8, 9. Key fields in OOM log: 'invoked oom-killer' line shows gfp_mask (allocation flags), order (page allocation order, 0 = single page), oom_score_adj of triggering process. Mem-Info section: active_anon, inactive_anon (anonymous memory pages), active_file, inactive_file (file cache pages), free (free pages). Node zone info: free vs min/low/high watermarks; OOM triggers when free < min. Swap info: SwapTotal and SwapFree; SwapFree=0 means swap fully exhausted. Task table: pid, uid, tgid, total_vm (virtual), rss (resident), pgtables_bytes, swapents (pages in swap), oom_score_adj, name. Kill line: 'Out of memory: Killed process PID (name) total-vm:XkB, anon-rss:XkB, file-rss:XkB'. Constraint field: CONSTRAINT_NONE means global OOM; CONSTRAINT_MEMCG means cgroup limit hit. Resolution requires Red Hat subscription for full access.

title: How to configure panic_on_oom in RHEL for HA Clusters
raw_text: Issue: How to configure a Red Hat Enterprise Linux system to panic and reboot on OOM instead of killing processes. Useful for High Availability clusters where a clean reboot and failover is preferred over unpredictable process killing. Environment: Red Hat Enterprise Linux 5, 6, 7, 8, 9. Configuration: set vm.panic_on_oom=1 in /etc/sysctl.conf. This makes the kernel panic when OOM occurs instead of invoking OOM killer. Combined with kernel.panic=N (where N is seconds to wait before reboot), system will automatically reboot after OOM panic. Example: vm.panic_on_oom=1 and kernel.panic=10 means system panics on OOM and reboots after 10 seconds. In HA cluster with watchdog or STONITH fencing, the panic triggers a fence event and failover to surviving node. Note: vm.panic_on_oom=1 does NOT panic for memcg-constrained OOM; use vm.panic_on_oom=2 to panic on all OOM events including cgroup. Resolution requires Red Hat subscription for full access.

title: Understanding and configuring memory cgroup OOM behavior in RHEL
raw_text: Issue: Processes killed by OOM even though system has plenty of free memory. This occurs because memory cgroup (memcg) limits restrict per-group memory independently of system total. Environment: Red Hat Enterprise Linux 7, 8, 9. Containerized environments (podman, docker, Kubernetes). Key concepts: cgroup memory limit is independent of system-wide memory. Log signature: 'Task in /cgroup/path killed as a result of limit of /cgroup/path' and 'memory: usage XkB, limit XkB, failcnt N'. When usage equals limit, cgroup OOM triggers regardless of system free memory. failcnt shows cumulative count of allocation failures against limit; high failcnt means sustained memory pressure within cgroup. Diagnosis: cat /sys/fs/cgroup/memory/<path>/memory.limit_in_bytes to check limit; cat memory.usage_in_bytes to check current usage; cat memory.failcnt to check failure count. Fix: increase cgroup memory limit to at least 1.2-1.5x of peak usage; or investigate application memory leak. For Kubernetes: increase container resource limits in pod spec. Resolution requires Red Hat subscription for full access.

title: How the OOM Killer selects which process to kill - Selection Logic in RHEL
raw_text: Issue: Understanding the selection logic of the OOM Killer. Why did OOM kill my important process instead of a less critical one? Environment: Red Hat Enterprise Linux 5, 6, 7, 8, 9. OOM selection algorithm overview: 1) If constraint is memcg, only processes within that cgroup are candidates. 2) For global OOM, all user processes are candidates except those with oom_score_adj=-1000. 3) oom_badness() calculates score for each candidate: score = (RSS + swap_usage + page_table_pages) * 1000 / totalpages, then adjusted by oom_score_adj. 4) Process with highest score selected. 5) If victim has children sharing same mm, all are killed. Protect critical processes: set oom_score_adj=-1000 for databases, critical services. Set high oom_score_adj (e.g., 1000) for expendable processes like batch jobs. For systemd: OOMScoreAdjust=-1000 in service unit. Verify: cat /proc/PID/oom_score shows current calculated score (0 means protected). Resolution requires Red Hat subscription for full access.

title: RHEL 8 Real-Time: Managing Out of Memory States - panic_on_oom Configuration
raw_text: Out-of-memory (OOM) is a computing state where all available memory, including swap space, has been allocated. Normally this causes the system to panic and stop functioning as expected. The /proc/sys/vm/panic_on_oom file contains a value which is the switch that controls OOM behavior. When the file contains 1, the kernel panics on OOM and stops functioning as expected. The default value is 0, which instructs the kernel to call the oom_killer() function when the system is in an OOM state. Usually, oom_killer() terminates unnecessary processes, which allows the system to survive. Procedure to change panic_on_oom: 1) Display current value: cat /proc/sys/vm/panic_on_oom. 2) Echo new value: echo 1 > /proc/sys/vm/panic_on_oom. 3) Verify: cat /proc/sys/vm/panic_on_oom. It is recommended that you make the Real-Time kernel panic on OOM (1). Otherwise, when the system encounters an OOM state, it is no longer deterministic.

title: RHEL 8 Real-Time: Prioritizing OOM Kill Targets with oom_score_adj
raw_text: You can prioritize the processes that get terminated by the oom_killer() function. This can ensure that high-priority processes keep running during an OOM state. Each process has a directory /proc/PID containing: oom_score_adj — valid scores in the range -16 to +15 (note: this is the RHEL RT doc's description; the actual Linux kernel range is -1000 to 1000). This value is used to calculate the performance footprint of the process, using an algorithm that also takes into account how long the process has been running, among other factors. oom_score — contains the result of the algorithm calculated using the value in oom_score_adj. In an OOM state, the oom_killer() function terminates processes with the highest oom_score. Procedure: 1) Display current oom_score: cat /proc/PID/oom_score. 2) Display current oom_score_adj: cat /proc/PID/oom_score_adj. 3) Edit value to lower priority: echo -5 > /proc/PID/oom_score_adj. 4) Verify new oom_score is lower. You can disable the oom_killer for a process by setting oom_score_adj to -17 (legacy) or -1000 (modern kernels).

title: RHEL 6 Performance Tuning Guide: Virtual Memory Tuning for OOM Prevention
raw_text: Virtual memory is typically consumed by processes, file system caches, and the kernel. Virtual memory utilization depends on a number of factors, which can be affected by the following parameters. swappiness: A value from 0 to 100 which controls the degree to which the system favors anonymous memory or the page cache. A high value improves file-system performance, while aggressively swapping less active processes out of physical memory. A low value avoids swapping processes out of memory, which usually decreases latency, at the cost of I/O performance. The default value is 60. Warning: Since RHEL 6.4, setting swappiness=0 more aggressively avoids swapping out, which increases the risk of OOM killing under strong memory and I/O pressure. A low swappiness value is recommended for database workloads. For example, for Oracle databases, Red Hat recommends a swappiness value of 10 (vm.swappiness=10). min_free_kbytes: The minimum number of kilobytes to keep free across the system. This value is used to compute a watermark value for each low memory zone, which are then assigned a number of reserved free pages proportional to their size. Warning: Be cautious when setting this parameter, as both too-low and too-high values can be damaging and break your system. Setting min_free_kbytes too low prevents the system from reclaiming memory, resulting in system hangs and OOM-killing multiple processes. Setting this parameter to a value that is too high (5-10% of total system memory) will cause your system to become out-of-memory immediately, as Linux is designed to use all available RAM to cache file system data, and setting a high min_free_kbytes value results in the system spending too much time reclaiming memory. dirty_ratio: Defines a percentage value. Writeout of dirty data begins (via pdflush) when dirty data comprises this percentage of total system memory. The default value is 20. Red Hat recommends a slightly lower value of 15 for database workloads. dirty_background_ratio: Defines a percentage value. Writeout of dirty data begins in the background (via pdflush) when dirty data comprises this percentage of total memory. The default value is 10. For database workloads, Red Hat recommends a lower value of 3. dirty_expire_centisecs: Specifies the number of centiseconds (hundredths of a second) dirty data remains in the page cache before it is eligible to be written back to disk. Red Hat does not recommend tuning this parameter. dirty_writeback_centisecs: Specifies the length of the interval between kernel flusher threads waking and writing eligible data to disk, in centiseconds (hundredths of a second). Setting this to 0 disables periodic write behavior. Red Hat does not recommend tuning this parameter. drop_caches: Setting this value to 1, 2, or 3 causes the kernel to drop various combinations of page cache and slab cache. 1: The system invalidates and frees all page cache memory. 2: The system frees all unused slab cache memory. 3: The system frees all page cache and slab cache memory. This is a non-destructive operation. Since dirty objects cannot be freed, running sync before setting this parameter's value is recommended. Using drop_caches to free memory is not recommended in a production environment. To set these values temporarily during tuning, echo the desired value to the appropriate file in the proc file system (e.g., echo 50 > /proc/sys/vm/swappiness). To set this value persistently, use the sysctl command.

title: RHEL 6 Resource Management Guide: cgroup Memory OOM Control and Notifications
raw_text: memory.oom_control contains a flag (0 or 1) that enables or disables the Out of Memory killer for a cgroup. If enabled (0), tasks that attempt to consume more memory than they are allowed are immediately killed by the OOM killer. The OOM killer is enabled by default in every cgroup using the memory subsystem; to disable it, write 1 to the memory.oom_control file: echo 1 > /cgroup/memory/lab1/memory.oom_control. When the OOM killer is disabled, tasks that attempt to use more memory than they are allowed are paused until additional memory is freed. The memory.oom_control file also reports the OOM status of the current cgroup under the under_oom entry. If the cgroup is out of memory and tasks in it are paused, the under_oom entry reports the value 1. The memory.oom_control file is capable of reporting an occurrence of an OOM situation using the notification API. Important: Processes in a cgroup that does not have the memory.memsw.limit_in_bytes parameter set can potentially use up all the available swap (after exhausting the set memory limitation) and trigger an Out Of Memory situation caused by the lack of available swap. OOM Control and Notifications example procedure: 1) Attach the memory subsystem to a hierarchy and create a cgroup: mount -t cgroup -o memory memory /cgroup/memory; mkdir /cgroup/memory/blue. 2) Set the amount of memory which tasks in the blue cgroup can use to 100 MB: echo 104857600 > memory.limit_in_bytes. 3) Confirm OOM killer is enabled: cat memory.oom_control shows oom_kill_disable 0, under_oom 0. 4) Move the current shell process into the tasks file: echo $$ > tasks. 5) Start a test program that attempts to allocate memory exceeding the limit. As soon as the blue cgroup runs out of free memory, the OOM killer kills the test program and reports Killed to standard output. 6) Disable the OOM killer and rerun the test program: echo 1 > memory.oom_control. This time, the test program remains paused waiting for additional memory to be freed, and under_oom changes to 1. Reenabling the OOM killer immediately kills the test program. 7) To receive notifications about every OOM situation, create an eventfd-based notification handler program and run it specifying the cgroup's cgroup.event_control and memory.oom_control as arguments; the handler reports "mem_cgroup oom event received" to standard output when OOM occurs.

title: RHEL 7 Real-Time Tuning Guide: Swapping and Out of Memory Tips
raw_text: Memory Swapping: Swapping pages out to disk can introduce latency in any environment. To ensure low latency, the best strategy is to have enough memory in your systems so that swapping is not necessary. Always size the physical RAM as appropriate for your application and system. Use vmstat to monitor memory usage and watch the si (swap in) and so (swap out) fields. It is optimal that they remain zero as much as possible. Out of Memory (OOM) refers to a computing state where all available memory, including swap space, has been allocated. Normally this will cause the system to panic and stop functioning as expected. There is a switch that controls OOM behavior in /proc/sys/vm/panic_on_oom. When set to 1 the kernel will panic on OOM. The default setting is 0 which instructs the kernel to call a function named oom_killer on an OOM. Usually, oom_killer can kill rogue processes and the system will survive. The easiest way to change this is to echo the new value to /proc/sys/vm/panic_on_oom: cat /proc/sys/vm/panic_on_oom → 0; echo 1 > /proc/sys/vm/panic_on_oom; cat /proc/sys/vm/panic_on_oom → 1. It is recommended that you make the Real-Time kernel panic on OOM. When the system has encountered an OOM state, it is no longer deterministic. It is also possible to prioritize which processes get killed by adjusting the oom_killer score. In /proc/PID/ there are two files named oom_adj and oom_score. Valid scores for oom_adj are in the range -16 to +15. This value is used to calculate the 'badness' of the process using an algorithm that also takes into account how long the process has been running, among other factors. To see the current oom_killer score, view the oom_score for the process. oom_killer will kill processes with the highest scores first. Example adjusting the oom_score of a process with a PID of 12465: cat /proc/12465/oom_score → 79872; echo -5 > /proc/12465/oom_adj; cat /proc/12465/oom_score → 78. There is also a special value of -17, which disables oom_killer for that process: cat /proc/12465/oom_score → 78; echo -17 > /proc/12465/oom_adj; cat /proc/12465/oom_score → 0, indicating that this process would not be killed. Related Manual Pages: swapon(2), swapon(8), vmstat(8).

title: RHEL 7 Real-Time: Using mlock Carefully to Avoid OOM Errors
raw_text: The mlock and mlockall system calls tell the system to lock to a specified memory range, and to not allow that memory to be paged. This means that once the physical page has been allocated to the page table entry, references to that page will always be fast. There are two groups of mlock system calls available. The mlock and munlock calls lock and unlock a specific range of addresses. The mlockall and munlockall calls lock or unlock the entire program space. Examine the use of mlock carefully and exercise caution. If the application is large, or if it has a large data domain, the mlock calls can cause thrashing if the system cannot allocate memory for other tasks. Always use mlock with care. Using it excessively can lead to an out of memory (OOM) error. Do not put an mlockall call at the start of your application. It is recommended that only the data and text of the realtime portion of the application be locked. Use of mlock will not guarantee that the program will experience no page I/O. It is used to ensure that the data will stay in memory, but cannot ensure that it will stay in the same page. Other functions such as move_pages and memory compactors can move data around despite the use of mlock. Unprivileged users must have the CAP_IPC_LOCK capability in order to be able to use mlockall or mlock on large buffers. Memory locks are made on a page basis, and do not stack. If two dynamically allocated memory segments share the same page locked twice by calls to mlock or mlockall, they will be unlocked by a single call to munlock for the corresponding page, or by munlockall. The two most common alternatives to mitigate the double-lock/single-unlock problem are: tracking the memory areas allocated and locked, and creating a wrapper function that before unlocking a page verifies how many users that page has (resource counting principle used in device drivers); performing allocations considering the page size and alignment to prevent a double-lock in the same page. The best way to use mlock depends on the application's needs and system resources. Example using mlock: the function alloc_workbuf dynamically allocates a memory buffer and locks it using posix_memalign for page alignment and mlock to lock the buffer into RAM; free_workbuf unlocks via munlock and frees the memory. Alternatively, it is possible to allocate and lock a memory area using mmap with the MAP_LOCKED flag, which allocates memory on a page basis and prevents the double-lock/single-unlock problem; however, if the size variable is not a multiple of the page size, the rest of the page is wasted, and a call to munlockall unlocks the memory locked by mmap. Another possibility for small-footprint applications is to call mlockall prior to entering a time-sensitive region of the code, followed by munlockall at the end, to reduce paging while in the critical section. Similarly, mlock can be used on a data region that is relatively static or that will grow slowly but needs to be accessed without page I/O.

title: RHEL 7 Kernel Admin Guide: kdump crashkernel OOM and Memory Reservation
raw_text: kdump is a service which provides a crash dumping mechanism using the kexec system call to boot into a second kernel (capture kernel) without rebooting, then captures the contents of the crashed kernel's memory and saves it into a file. The second kernel resides in a reserved part of the system memory. Memory reserved for the kdump kernel is always reserved during system boot via the crashkernel= boot parameter. The crashkernel=auto value enables automatic configuration of reserved memory based on the total amount of memory in the system. Replace the auto value with a specific amount to change this behavior (e.g., crashkernel=128M). Variable memory reservation syntax is also supported: crashkernel=512M-2G:64M,2G-:128M reserves 64 MB if total system memory is 512 MB to 2 GB, and 128 MB if more than 2 GB. Memory offset can also be specified (e.g., crashkernel=128M@16M). If drivers bnx2x and bmx2fc are not blacklisted in the kdump kernel then the second kernel leads to panic and the dumps will not be captured. Blacklisting kernel drivers prevents the oom killer or other crash kernel failures. To blacklist, update the KDUMP_COMMANDLINE_APPEND= variable in /etc/sysconfig/kdump with rd.driver.blacklist=<modules> or modprobe.blacklist=<modules>, then restart the kdump service with systemctl restart kdump. For firmware-assisted dump (fadump) on IBM PowerPC, if you observe Out of Memory (OOM) errors when booting from the crash kernel, increase the value specified in crashkernel= until the crash kernel can boot cleanly. Some trial and error may be required in this case. When dumping to a software FCoE target, you can encounter Out of Memory (OOM) issue. In such cases, increase the default crashkernel=auto parameter value.

title: RHEL 10 Real-Time: Managing OOM States and Process Priority Configuration
raw_text: Managing Out of Memory states. Out-of-memory (OOM) is a computing state where all available memory, including swap space, has been allocated. Normally this causes the system to panic and stop functioning as expected. The provided instructions help in avoiding OOM states on your system. Prerequisites: You have root permissions on the system. 21.2. Changing the Out of Memory value: The /proc/sys/vm/panic_on_oom file contains a value which is the switch that controls Out of Memory (OOM) behavior. When the file contains 1, the kernel panics on OOM and stops functioning as expected. The default value is 0, which instructs the kernel to call the oom_killer() function when the system is in an OOM state. Usually, oom_killer() terminates unnecessary processes, which allows the system to survive. Procedure: 1) Display the current value: cat /proc/sys/vm/panic_on_oom → 0. 2) Echo the new value: echo 1 > /proc/sys/vm/panic_on_oom. Note: It is recommended that you make the Real-Time kernel panic on OOM (1). Otherwise, when the system encounters an OOM state, it is no longer deterministic. Verification: cat /proc/sys/vm/panic_on_oom → 1; verify that the displayed value matches the value specified. 21.3. Prioritizing processes to end when in an Out of Memory state: You can prioritize which processes the oom_killer() function ends by adjusting the oom_score_adj value for each process, ensuring that high-priority processes keep running during an OOM state. Each process has a directory at /proc/PID/ that contains the following files: oom_score_adj — valid scores are in the range -16 to +15, this value is used to calculate the performance footprint of the process, using an algorithm that also takes into account how long the process has been running, among other factors; oom_score — contains the result of the algorithm calculated using the value in oom_score_adj. In an OOM state, the oom_killer() function ends processes with the highest oom_score. You can prioritize which processes are ended by editing the oom_score_adj file for the process. Prerequisites: Know the process ID (PID) of the process you want to prioritize. Procedure: 1) Display the current oom_score: cat /proc/12465/oom_score → 79872. 2) Display the contents of oom_score_adj: cat /proc/12465/oom_score_adj → 13. 3) Edit the value in oom_score_adj: echo -5 > /proc/12465/oom_score_adj. Verification: cat /proc/12465/oom_score → 78; verify that the displayed value is lower than the previous value. 21.4. Disabling the Out of Memory killer for a process: You can disable the oom_killer() function for a process by setting oom_score_adj to the reserved value of -17. This will keep the process alive, even in an OOM state. Procedure: echo -17 > /proc/12465/oom_score_adj. Verification: cat /proc/12465/oom_score → 0; verify that the displayed value is 0.

title: Linux Overcommit Accounting — vm.overcommit_memory
raw_text: Overcommit Accounting: The Linux kernel supports the following overcommit handling modes. 0: Heuristic overcommit handling. Obvious overcommits of address space are refused. Used for a typical system. It ensures a seriously wild allocation fails while allowing overcommit to reduce swap usage. This is the default. 1: Always overcommit. Appropriate for some scientific applications. Classic example is code using sparse arrays and just relying on the virtual memory consisting almost entirely of zero pages. 2: Don't overcommit. The total address space commit for the system is not permitted to exceed swap + a configurable amount (default is 50%) of physical RAM. Depending on the amount you use, in most situations this means a process will not be killed while accessing pages but will receive errors on memory allocation as appropriate. Useful for applications that want to guarantee their memory allocations will be available in the future without having to initialize every page. The overcommit policy is set via the sysctl vm.overcommit_memory. The overcommit amount can be set via vm.overcommit_ratio (percentage) or vm.overcommit_kbytes (absolute value). These only have an effect when vm.overcommit_memory is set to 2. The current overcommit limit and amount committed are viewable in /proc/meminfo as CommitLimit and Committed_AS respectively. Gotchas: The C language stack growth does an implicit mremap. If you want absolute guarantees and run close to the edge you MUST mmap your stack for the largest size you think you will need. For typical stack usage this does not matter much but it's a corner case if you really really care. In mode 2 the MAP_NORESERVE flag is ignored. How It Works: The overcommit is based on the following rules. For a file backed map: SHARED or READ-only - 0 cost (the file is the map not swap); PRIVATE WRITABLE - size of mapping per instance. For an anonymous or /dev/zero map: SHARED - size of mapping; PRIVATE READ-only - 0 cost (but of little use); PRIVATE WRITABLE - size of mapping per instance. Additional accounting: Pages made writable copies by mmap; shmfs memory drawn from the same pool. Status: We account mmap memory mappings; We account mprotect changes in commit; We account mremap changes in size; We account brk; We account munmap; We report the commit status in /proc; Account and check on fork; Review stack handling/building on exec; SHMfs accounting; Implement actual limit enforcement. To Do: Account ptrace pages (this is hard).

title: Overcommit Accounting - Linux Kernel Documentation
raw_text: Linux kernel supports three overcommit modes via vm.overcommit_memory. Mode 0 (default): heuristic, refuses obvious overcommits while reducing swap usage. Mode 1: always overcommit, for scientific apps using sparse arrays of mostly zero pages. Mode 2: strict, total virtual address space commit not permitted to exceed swap plus configurable percentage (default 50 percent) of physical RAM. Overcommit amount set via vm.overcommit_ratio (percentage) or vm.overcommit_kbytes (absolute). Current overcommit limit and committed amount viewable in /proc/meminfo as CommitLimit and Committed_AS. In mode 2 MAP_NORESERVE flag is ignored. File-backed shared or read-only maps cost 0; private writable maps cost size of mapping per instance. C language stack growth does implicit mremap; run close to edge means you must mmap your stack for the largest size you think you will need.

title: Documentation for /proc/sys/vm/ - Key OOM-Related Parameters
raw_text: Key OOM-related /proc/sys/vm parameters: admin_reserve_kbytes defaults to min(3 percent free pages, 8MB) to reserve memory for root login and process kill under pressure. min_free_kbytes forces kernel to keep minimum kilobytes free, computes WMARK_MIN per lowmem zone; below 1024KB causes deadlocks under high load; too high causes immediate OOM. oom_dump_tasks: if enabled dumps per-task info (pid, uid, vm size, rss, pgtables, swapents, oom_score_adj, name) on OOM event. oom_kill_allocating_task: set to 1 to kill the task that triggered OOM instead of scanning all processes. panic_on_oom: 0 kills process and continues (default), 1 panics except for mempolicy/cpuset constrained cases, 2 always panics. swappiness: 0-200, lower values prefer page cache reclaim over swapping anonymous memory; 10 recommended for database servers. overcommit_memory: 0/1/2 controls policy. user_reserve_kbytes: reserves min(3 percent of process size, 128MB) in mode 2 to prevent user from starting a single memory hogging process that prevents recovery.

title: Memory Resource Controller - cgroup v1 Memory Documentation
raw_text: cgroup v1 memory controller isolates memory behavior between task groups. memory.limit_in_bytes sets hard memory usage limit. memory.memsw.limit_in_bytes limits combined memory and swap. memory.soft_limit_in_bytes sets soft limit, triggers reclaim when system needs memory. memory.oom_control: writing 1 disables OOM killer for the cgroup so tasks receive SIGKILL instead; exposes oom_kill_disable, under_oom, and oom_kill counter fields. OOM troubleshooting within cgroup: cgroup limit too low; anonymous memory used with swap off or too low. sync then echo 1 > /proc/sys/vm/drop_caches helps clear page cache. memory.failcnt counts failed allocation attempts. memory.stat provides detailed breakdown: cache, rss, mapped_file, pgfault, pgmajfault, swap. When cgroup OOM occurs kernel logs: Task in /cgroup/path killed as a result of limit of /cgroup/path. memory.swappiness overrides global swappiness for the group; setting 0 truly prevents any swapping in cgroup even with swap available, which may cause memcg OOM if no file pages to reclaim.

title: Out Of Memory Management - Understanding Linux Virtual Memory (Gorman)
raw_text: OOM manager task: check if enough memory exists, verify system is truly OOM, and select a process to kill. vm_enough_memory() checks available memory by summing: total page cache, free pages, free swap pages, slab reclaimable pages. Allowed threshold = TotalRam * (OverCommitRatio/100) + TotalSwapPages. OOM detection heuristics: enough swap space left (nr_swap_pages > 0) means not OOM; more than 5 seconds since last failure means not OOM; fewer than 10 failures in the last 5 seconds means not OOM; a process was killed in the last 5 seconds means not OOM. select_bad_process() steps through each running task calling badness(). badness formula: total_vm / (sqrt(cpu_time_seconds) * sqrt(sqrt(cpu_time_minutes))). Selects process using large memory but not long-lived. out_of_memory() logs victim info and sends SIGKILL. VM_ACCOUNT flag on VMAs tracks committed space to avoid OOM where possible.

title: Linux Memory Management Concepts Overview - Zones, Reclaim, OOM Killer
raw_text: Linux memory managed in zones (ZONE_DMA, ZONE_DMA32, ZONE_NORMAL, ZONE_HIGHMEM on 32-bit). Physical memory divided into page frames; virtual memory abstracts physical details from applications. Pages classified as reclaimable (page cache, anonymous memory) or unreclaimable (kernel data, DMA buffers). Reclaim: kernel frees reclaimable pages when needed; clean page cache freed immediately, dirty pages written to disk first, anonymous pages swapped out. OOM killer invoked when kernel unable to reclaim enough memory to continue. OOM killer selects a task to sacrifice for overall system health. Selected task killed in hope that after exit enough memory freed to continue normal operation. Huge pages reduce TLB pressure and improve performance; mlock and hugepages are unreclaimable, overusing them risks OOM. Compaction reduces memory fragmentation by moving movable pages to consolidate free pages.

title: cgroup v2 Memory Controller - memory.max, memory.high, memory.oom.group
raw_text: cgroup v2 memory controls: memory.min: hard memory protection, cgroup memory will not be reclaimed under any conditions within this boundary; if no unprotected reclaimable memory available OOM killer invoked. memory.high: throttle limit, processes throttled under heavy reclaim pressure when exceeded but OOM killer never invoked; over-committing on high limit is a viable management strategy. memory.max: hard limit, OOM killer invoked when usage reaches limit and cannot be reduced. memory.oom.group: if set all tasks in cgroup killed together to guarantee workload integrity, tasks with oom_score_adj=-1000 are exception and never killed. memory.events: tracks oom_kill count, group_oom count, max and high breach counters. memory.pressure: shows PSI stall information for the cgroup. O_NONBLOCK on memory.max bypasses synchronous reclaim and oom-kill for admin processes dynamically adjusting limits. memory.swap.max limits swap usage; swap.failcnt counts swap allocation failures.

title: How to Configure the Linux Out-of-Memory Killer - Oracle Technical Article
raw_text: Linux kernel allocates memory on demand and over-commits by default. OOM killer terminates processes when over-commitment becomes problematic. Finding OOM victims: grep -i kill /var/log/messages shows 'Out of Memory: Killed process PID (name)'. Capital K in Killed indicates SIGKILL signal. Monitor memory: free -lm shows high and low memory; vmstat -SM 45 10 shows memory every 45 seconds. On 64-bit platforms all memory shows as low memory since virtual addressing not needed. Long-term monitoring: vmstat -SM 120 1000 > memoryusage.out & Legacy oom_adj per process in /proc/PID/oom_adj range -17 to 15; value -17 fully protects process. Modern kernels use oom_score_adj range -1000 to 1000. Disable OOM killer system-wide: vm.overcommit_memory=2 with vm.overcommit_ratio=100 in /etc/sysctl.conf prevents over-commitment.

title: Teaching the OOM Killer About Control Groups - LWN.net (2018)
raw_text: cgroup-aware OOM killer introduced for Linux 4.19 development cycle by Roman Gushchin. Problem: single large process in cgroup A selected over 100 small processes in cgroup B even if B uses more memory overall. Solution: find control group with largest memory consumption first, then kill largest process within that group. New knob memory.oom_group: if set to non-zero kills all processes in targeted group simultaneously to guarantee workload integrity. Useful when processes in a group depend on each other and partial kill would cause failure anyway. Controversy: processes in root control group treated differently; oom_score_adj not used for processes inside non-root cgroups. OOM kill algorithm: not hierarchical, each control group considered as separate entity; child group with large usage selected even if parent group total is larger. OOM killer heuristics implemented in oom_badness(): sum memory used by process scaled by oom_score_adj value.

title: Toward More Predictable and Reliable OOM Handling - LWN.net (2015)
raw_text: Two patch sets from Michal Hocko to improve OOM detection and OOM killer reliability. OOM detection problem: no bounds on how long reclaimable pages take to actually be reclaimed; kernel can loop indefinitely in allocation attempts that do not succeed. New heuristic: upper bound of sixteen retries before entering OOM handling mode; after eight retries count of reclaimable pages discounted by half to avoid false hope. Result: more predictable OOM handling when memory gets tight. OOM reaper: problem where targeted process blocked on lock cannot exit and cannot free memory. OOM reaper is a kernel thread that strips victim process of anonymous memory before it exits; possible because process received SIGKILL so will not run again in user mode. OOM reaper merged for 4.6 kernel release. TIF_MEMDIE flag set on victim gives it priority for remaining memory allocations during exit cleanup.

title: User-Space Out-of-Memory Handling - LWN.net (2014)
raw_text: User-space OOM handler proposed by David Rientjes (Google) as alternative to kernel OOM killer. Kernel OOM killer can only kill a process; cannot examine memory leak, create debug artifacts, or select by custom policy. Approach: reserve small subset of min_free_kbytes for user-space OOM handler to allocate in OOM conditions. User-space handler can: read process list, check memory usage, implement custom kill policy, create debugging artifacts. Memory controller cgroup setup: CONFIG_MEMCG=y required; mount at /sys/fs/cgroup/memory. memcg interface: memory.limit_in_bytes, memory.usage_in_bytes, memory.oom_control for OOM event notification via eventfd. Register notifier: create eventfd, open memory.oom_control, write eventfd and fd to cgroup.event_control. User-space handler notified through eventfd when OOM happens; can then examine state and decide action. Future: library to implement HandleOOM() function with common OOM response strategies.

title: Taming the OOM Killer - LWN.net (2009)
raw_text: History and control mechanisms for OOM killer as of 2009. oom_adj knob introduced in /proc/PID/oom_adj to save important processes from being killed. Range -17 to 15; -17 fully exempts process from OOM killer. Overcommit allows system to use memory more efficiently but risks OOM situations. Memory-hogging programs can deplete system memory causing system halt. Situation when even single page cannot be allocated: OOM killer kicks in to select sacrificial process. mem_notify patch (Kosaki Motohiro): allows applications to monitor /dev/mem_notify to receive notifications when memory gets tight. Applications can then free caches before OOM killer is invoked. poll() or FASYNC signal can be used to monitor memory pressure device file. memory_pressure_notify() throttled to once per five seconds to reduce thundering herd problem. Various control approaches discussed: cpuset-aware OOM, user-space OOM notification, better heuristics.

title: Improving the OOM Killer - LSFMM 2016 - LWN.net
raw_text: Michal Hocko session at 2016 Linux Storage Filesystem Memory-Management Summit on OOM improvements. Goal: make OOM situation detection more reliable and deterministic. Current practice: reclaim memory until nothing more found for several iterations, then invoke OOM killer; buggy in practice. Feedback mechanism from reclaim and compaction code to invoke OOM killer when situation does not improve over time. Future goal: detect thrashing situations and start OOM killer proactively even before strictly out of memory. OOM reaper already merged for 4.6: deprives victim process of memory resources before it can exit, avoids deadlock on locks. Timeout debate: some developers want timeouts so OOM killer moves to new victim if first kill does not free memory within bounded time. Michal pushes back on timeouts preferring other means; OOM reaper his preferred alternative. Edge case: when all memory tied up in tmpfs filesystem (no backing store), killing processes does not free memory; Google experiment to truncate large tmpfs files when OOM but considered dangerous.

title: Managing Kernel Resources - PostgreSQL Documentation (OOM Configuration)
raw_text: PostgreSQL postmaster process may be terminated by OOM killer when memory demands cause system to run out of virtual memory. Log message: 'Out of Memory: Killed process 12345 (postgres)'. When postmaster killed: existing connections continue but no new connections accepted; PostgreSQL must be restarted. Recommended fix 1: set vm.overcommit_memory=2 in /etc/sysctl.conf to prevent over-commitment and lower OOM chances. Recommended fix 2: set OOM score adjustment for postmaster to -1000 to guarantee it is never targeted. Command in startup script before invoking postgres: echo -1000 > /proc/self/oom_score_adj Then set environment variables: PG_OOM_ADJUST_FILE=/proc/self/oom_score_adj and PG_OOM_ADJUST_VALUE=0 so that postmaster child processes run with normal OOM score of zero and remain killable by OOM. Memory tuning: reduce shared_buffers, work_mem, hash_mem_multiplier; reduce max_connections and use connection pooling. Increasing swap space helps because OOM killer invoked only when both physical memory and swap exhausted.

title: Linux OOM Killer: A Detailed Guide to Memory Management - Last9
raw_text: OOM Killer activates when both RAM and swap exhausted and memory allocation requests cannot be satisfied. Badness score factors: memory footprint (more memory equals higher score), process runtime (longer running gets lower score as likely important), root privileges (root processes penalized less), nice value (higher nice equals higher score), oom_score_adj override. Sacrifice child principle: kernel prefers killing child processes before parent for stability. In containerized environments a single memory leak can trigger cascading OOM kills across cluster. OOM kills appear as mysterious container restarts or service degradations in production. Monitor with: watch -n 1 cat /proc/meminfo showing MemFree MemAvailable SwapFree. Use dmesg --ctime and grep oom for timestamped OOM events. Production debugging: kernel logs OOM decisions to /var/log/messages or system journal but logs get buried under other events.

title: What is vm.min_free_kbytes and How to Tune It - LinuxHint
raw_text: vm.min_free_kbytes forces kernel memory manager to keep minimum kilobytes free. Computes WMARK_MIN watermark per lowmem zone proportionally by zone size. Below 1024KB: system becomes subtly broken and prone to deadlocks under high loads. Too high: causes immediate OOM. Testing: setting to 1500000 on 3.75GB RAM system reduces MemAvailable by 1.5GB but MemFree unchanged because kernel reserves but does not use the memory. Buffer cache cannot use reserved memory so a 2GB file cannot fully fit in cache when min_free_kbytes is large. To set temporarily: echo VALUE > /proc/sys/vm/min_free_kbytes then verify with sysctl vm.min_free_kbytes. To set permanently: add vm.min_free_kbytes = VALUE to /etc/sysctl.conf then run sysctl -p. Recommended: keep near default about 1 percent of RAM; increase to 1 to 2 percent for high-load servers to prevent OOM deadlocks from dirty page writeout itself requiring memory allocation.

title: How to Analyze Linux oom-killer Logs - LinuxVox
raw_text: Guide for analyzing OOM killer logs on Ubuntu with 1GB RAM and no swap. Extract OOM logs: dmesg | grep -i oom or journalctl -k | grep oom. Complete log format: invoked oom-killer line with gfp_mask and order fields; Mem-Info section with active/inactive anon/file pages; per-task table with pid, uid, tgid, total_vm, rss, pgtables_bytes, swapents, oom_score_adj, name; victim selection line: Out of memory: Kill process PID (name) score SCORE or sacrifice child; confirmation line: Killed process PID (name) total-vm:XkB, anon-rss:XkB, file-rss:XkB. Root cause categories: memory leak (RSS grows continuously), undersized system, missing swap, overcommit misconfiguration. Parse oom_score column in task table to understand why specific process selected. Prevention: add swap, tune swappiness, set cgroup memory limits, fix application memory leaks.

title: Dealing with the Linux OOM Killer at Program and OS Level - GitHub Gist
raw_text: Program-level OOM protection for single process on shared machine: oom_score_adj in /proc/PID/oom_score_adj ranges -1000 to 1000, default 0. Set -1000 to prevent kill even at 100 percent memory usage: sudo echo -1000 > /proc/PID/oom_score_adj. Normal users can increase score but need root to decrease. OS-level disable for dedicated server: 1) disable OOM killer with vm.oom-kill=0 in /etc/sysctl.conf; 2) disable memory overcommit with vm.overcommit_memory=2 in /etc/sysctl.conf where 0 means estimate if enough RAM, 1 means always say yes, 2 means say no if not enough memory. Default Linux allows more allocation than system has then randomly kills processes under pressure. Disabling recommended when machine used specifically for single application such as cloud VM. Gist has 37 stars indicating community validation. Warning: OS-level disable risks system hang when memory truly exhausted and no process killable.

title: Stack Overflow OOM Q&A: Process Protection, Swap Addition, Container OOM, swappiness Tuning
raw_text: Selected high-vote Q&A from Stack Overflow OOM tag. Q: Protect critical process from OOM killer. A: echo -1000 > /proc/PID/oom_score_adj (requires root). For systemd: OOMScoreAdjust=-1000 under [Service]. Verify: cat /proc/PID/oom_score shows 0. Q: Docker container exits with code 137 repeatedly. A: Exit code 137 equals 128+9 (SIGKILL), indicates OOM kill. Check docker inspect for OOMKilled:true. Set limit: docker run --memory=512m. Q: Adding swap to prevent OOM. A: fallocate -l 4G /swapfile then chmod 600 then mkswap then swapon. Make permanent via /etc/fstab: /swapfile none swap sw 0 0. Q: Best swappiness for database server. A: swappiness 10 recommended for MySQL PostgreSQL Redis. Default 60 too aggressive for servers. Change: sysctl -w vm.swappiness=10. Q: Detecting memory leak causing repeated OOM. A: Monitor RSS growth: ps -p PID -o rss over time. If growing continuously suspect leak. Use valgrind or AddressSanitizer. Check /proc/PID/maps line count for mmap leak.

title: PSI - Pressure Stall Information - Linux Kernel Documentation
raw_text: PSI (Pressure Stall Information) quantifies disruptions caused by CPU, memory, and IO resource crunches before OOM occurs. Memory pressure exported via /proc/pressure/memory with two lines: some: share of time at least some tasks stalled on memory; full: share of time all non-idle tasks stalled simultaneously (system thrashing, actual CPU cycles wasted). Register threshold trigger to monitor: write threshold format to /proc/pressure/memory, e.g. 'some 150000 1000000' adds 150ms threshold within 1 second window. Use poll() or select() on the file descriptor to wait for events. Trigger deregisters when file descriptor closed. Per-cgroup PSI: memory.pressure file in cgroupfs mountpoint; same format as /proc/pressure/memory. PSI allows proactive action before OOM: load shedding, migrating jobs, killing low-priority batch jobs. Maximizes hardware utilization without risking OOM kills. Notifications rate-limited to one per tracking window to avoid excessive wakeups.

title: Memory Allocation Guide - GFP Flags and OOM Killer Interaction
raw_text: Linux memory allocation APIs use GFP flags to control allocation behavior including OOM killer invocation. GFP_KERNEL: default, both background and direct reclaim allowed; not-costly allocations basically no-fail but OOM killer victim allowed to fail currently. GFP_KERNEL | __GFP_NORETRY: fails early rather than cause disruptive reclaim (one round); OOM killer NOT invoked; suitable when failure can be handled at small cost such as reduced throughput. GFP_KERNEL | __GFP_RETRY_MAYFAIL: tries really hard, retries if progress made elsewhere; OOM killer NOT triggered; use for caches with associated shrinker. GFP_KERNEL | __GFP_NOFAIL: loops endlessly until success; dangerous especially for larger orders. GFP_ATOMIC (non-sleeping, interrupt context): cannot sleep or wait; allocation likely to fail under memory pressure; callers must handle failure. GFP_NOWAIT: prevents direct reclaim and IO/filesystem operations; suitable for interrupt handler path. gfp_mask field in OOM log indicates allocation type that triggered OOM; GFP_ATOMIC failures in interrupt context are non-fatal page allocation failures not true OOM.

title: How to Find Which Process Was Killed by Linux OOM Killer - Baeldung
raw_text: Linux, when used with the default settings, will overcommit memory. On one hand, this improves resource utilization. On the other hand, this is dangerous and cannot be directly controlled by applications. That is why we need to implement analysis and recovery rather than try to mitigate the error. We can configure Linux to use different policies to allocate memory. We can find the effective policy by mounting the proc filesystem and reading from /proc/sys/vm/. The files overcommit_ratio and overcommit_memory govern how much memory the system may commit that is not backed by physical memory. OOM (out of memory) killer will only run if we configure the system to overcommit memory. If, however, we wanted to prevent the system from overcommitting we would set overcommit_memory to 2. In this case, the system would only overcommit total_memory * overcommit_ratio / 100. In other words, if we set overcommit_memory to 2 and overcommit_ratio to 0, the OOM killer would be disabled. Unfortunately, we need to rely on logs to find out about OOM errors. Linux has a whole zoo of different logging tools. journald reads the information about kernel events using syslog interface, typically, from a file in the proc filesystem: /proc/kmsg. Another popular program that reads this file is dmesg. journald is used because, unlike dmesg, it keeps the information available after the system reboot. journald is a daemon responsible for collecting and storing system logs. It has a sister program: journalctl for controlling it. Since journald stores structured logs, journalctl gives us plenty of tools to sift through them quickly. If we wanted to find the process id and the program name of the process that was killed by the OOM killer, we could run: journalctl --list-boots | awk '{ print $1 }' | xargs -I{} journalctl --utc --no-pager -b {} -kqg 'killed process' -o verbose --output-fields=MESSAGE. Example output shows: MESSAGE=Killed process 3029 (Web Content) total-vm:10206696kB, anon-rss:6584572kB, file-rss:0kB, shmem-rss:8732kB. And: MESSAGE=Killed process 131641 (bash) total-vm:11257404kB, anon-rss:11243152kB, file-rss:12kB, shmem-rss:0kB. The journalctl command options: --utc tells journalctl to convert dates to UTC, this is especially useful when dealing with systemd logs imported from other systems whose clock we cannot access, otherwise dealing with geographically diverse systems becomes very difficult. -b selects logs associated with a particular system boot, there is no way to select multiple boots in one call, unfortunately a reboot or even a series of reboots will often follow an OOM error. -k filters log messages using _TRANSPORT=kernel, in other words it filters the kernel events. -q instructs journalctl to omit technical information related to log display, this is useful in scripts searching for any OOM-related events. -g runs regular expression search in a manner similar to how search works in Emacs, if the expression contains no capital letters it is assumed to be case-insensitive. --list-boots prints all the boots for which journalctl has logs. -o verbose instructs journalctl to print a filtered list of fields associated with the message, alternatively -o json will produce JSON output for further processing in a log aggregation system. --output-fields is a comma-separated list of fields to be printed, journalctl will print some fields unconditionally, of particular interest are those appearing in the square brackets after the date which specifies the cursor that we can later supply with the -c option to open the logs in that location. To start examining the OOM log interactively: journalctl --utc -b -X -ke where X is an integer counting from the last boot backward while positive integers count from the first boot forward. Navigation commands in interactive mode use less key bindings. The -e option fast-forwards the pager to the end of the log. Next we use one of the available search commands, for example by typing ? followed by Killed. Once the cursor moves to the error message of interest, we can search again for Mem-info which will rewind the log to the summary of the state of the virtual memory at the time OOM killer ran, followed by the list of processes and statistics of their memory usage. Some Linux systems don't use journald. A popular tool to manage system logs on Linux is rsyslogd. This tool has a rich system of modules and a number of configuration languages. The entry point to rsyslog configuration is the file /etc/rsyslog.conf, usually accompanied by the /etc/rsyslog.d directory which adds more configuration files. Conceptually, rsyslog consists of these kinds of modules: inputs where the module name starts with im such as imfile for reading logs from arbitrary files, outputs where the module name starts with om such as ommysql for storing logs in MySQL database, and parser where the module name starts with pm such as pmlastmsg for dealing with repeating messages. A configuration rule for matching kernel messages typically looks like: kern.* -/var/log/kern.log. This instructs rsyslog to store messages coming from the kernel in a file called /var/log/kern.log. If this file is too long, rsyslog will typically rotate and archive it. To search kern.log for OOM kills: find /var/log -name kern* -exec grep -PnHe 'Killed process' {} + 2>/dev/null. Example output: /var/log/kern.log:5207:Jul 6 11:58:34 cheetah kernel: [401912.220043] Killed process 131641 (bash) total-vm:11257404kB, anon-rss:11243152kB, file-rss:12kB, shmem-rss:0kB. For archived logs: find /var/log -name kern*.gz -exec zgrep -PnHe 'Killed process' {} + 2>/dev/null. Example output shows multiple chrome processes killed: Killed process 98254 (chrome) total-vm:889684kB, anon-rss:113068kB, Killed process 102503 (chrome) total-vm:911152kB, anon-rss:104748kB, Killed process 96187 (chrome) total-vm:873296kB, anon-rss:103512kB, and Killed process 3029 (Web Content) total-vm:10206696kB, anon-rss:6584572kB. The modular structure of rsyslog means data may be sent to a centralized log collector, or to a local database server, or simply to a pseudoterminal. The choice of loggers is far greater than journald and rsyslogd. To find all programs potentially logging kernel events: sudo lsof | grep /dev/kmsg. This will list all programs that are reading this file and potentially storing its contents somewhere.

title: Linux Memory Overcommitment and the OOM Killer - Baeldung
raw_text: Linux operating systems have specific ways of managing memory. One of the policies is overcommitment, which allows applications to book in advance as much memory as it wants. However, the promised memory may not be available when it comes to its actual use. Then, the system must provide a special means to avoid running out of memory. The Out-Of-Memory (OOM) killer is a process that eliminates applications for the sake of system stability. For the OOM killer to work, the system must allow overcommitting. Each process is scored by how much the system would gain from eliminating it. When it comes to the low memory state, the kernel kills the process of the highest score. We can find the score of a process by its PID in the /proc/PID/oom_score file. For example, cat /proc/$$/oom_score returns the score of the current terminal process. The score takes values from 0 to 1000. A value of zero means the process is safe from the OOM killer. To list all processes sorted by oom_score, use a script that reads ps -e -o pid= -o comm= and prints pid, oom_score from /proc/PID/oom_score, and process name, sorted from lowest to highest. Example output shows system processes with score 0, desktop processes with scores in single digits, and applications like firefox with score 62 being the highest. To protect a process from the OOM killer, set the oom_score_adj parameter. The parameter takes values in the range from -1000 to 1000 inclusively. Its negative value decreases the oom_score, making the process less attractive for the killer. Positive values cause the score to rise. A process with oom_score_adj equal to -1000 is immune to the killing. Check the parameter in the file /proc/PID/oom_score_adj. Setting oom_score_adj by hand: we can write to the oom_score_adj file directly. For example checking Firefox's score: cat /proc/$(pgrep firefox)/oom_score returns 60. Making it more killer prone: echo 500 > /proc/$(pgrep firefox)/oom_score_adj causes the score to rise to 562. Increasing its safety: sudo echo -30 > /proc/$(pgrep firefox)/oom_score_adj causes the score to drop to 31. We need sudo privilege to decrease the adjustment factor below zero. The choom command (part of the util-linux package) can report the score and modify its adjustment. choom -p PID shows current OOM score and current OOM score adjust value. choom -p PID -n 300 changes the OOM score adjust value. With choom we can start a process right away with the given oom_score_adj: choom -n 300 firefox. For systemd services, we can permanently adjust the score in the service configuration located or linked in the /etc/systemd folder by editing the OOMScoreAdjust entry in the Service section. Example from snapd service configuration: OOMScoreAdjust=-900 in the Service section. How oom_score is calculated depends on the kernel version. Besides memory footprint, older releases might account for the running time, nice level, or root ownership. However, in kernel version 5, only the total memory usage matters. The function oom_badness in the oom_kill.c source file first checks if the process is immune, usually thanks to oom_score_adj equal to -1000, in which case the task obtains a zero score. Otherwise, the task's RAM, virtual memory, and swap space sizes are summed up. Then the result is divided by the total available memory. Finally, the function normalizes the ratio to 1000. At this moment, the oom_score_adj comes into play and adds to the score. Thus its negative value actually reduces this value. We can change the Linux system's overcommit policy by setting the overcommit_memory parameter in the /proc/sys/vm/overcommit_memory file. Value 0 allows moderate overcommit where unreasonable memory allocation will fail, this is the default setting. Value 1 always overcommits. Value 2 does not allow overcommit, a process usually will not be terminated by the OOM killer but the memory allocation attempts may return an error. Using policies other than the default one imposes greater requirements on the way the applications deal with memory. Demonstration on Ubuntu 20.04 LTS with kernel 5.4.0-58-generic with around 4 GB of memory and 4 GB of swap space: a test script allocating a lot of memory by appending strings in a loop. The system could maintain up to three test applications running simultaneously. Starting the fourth instance woke the OOM killer up. The terminal showed the message Killed. Looking for the corresponding event in kern.log with grep -ia "Killed process" kern.log showed: Out of memory: Killed process 20257 (test) total-vm:1980996kB, anon-rss:21128kB, file-rss:0kB, shmem-rss:0kB, UID:1000 pgtables:3920kB oom_score_adj:0. More information about PID 20257 showed: oom-kill:constraint=CONSTRAINT_NONE, nodemask=(null), cpuset=/, mems_allowed=0, global_oom, task_memcg=/user.slice/user-1000.slice/user@1000.service, task=test, pid=20257, uid=1000. The oom_reaper then reaped process 20257: now anon-rss:0kB, file-rss:0kB, shmem-rss:0kB. The process's last oom_score in the log file showed test processes with scores of 235 and 236 being the highest scored.

title: Docker Resource Constraints - Memory Limits and OOM Behavior
raw_text: By default, a container has no resource constraints and can use as much of a given resource as the host's kernel scheduler allows. Docker provides ways to control how much memory, or CPU a container can use, setting runtime configuration flags of the docker run command. It is important not to allow a running container to consume too much of the host machine's memory. On Linux hosts, if the kernel detects that there is not enough memory to perform important system functions, it throws an OOME, or Out Of Memory Exception, and starts killing processes to free up memory. Any process is subject to killing, including Docker and other important applications. This can effectively bring the entire system down if the wrong process is killed. Docker attempts to mitigate these risks by adjusting the OOM priority on the Docker daemon so that it is less likely to be killed than other processes on the system. The OOM priority on containers is not adjusted. This makes it more likely for an individual container to be killed than for the Docker daemon or other system processes to be killed. You should not try to circumvent these safeguards by manually setting --oom-score-adj to an extreme negative number on the daemon or a container, or by setting --oom-kill-disable on a container. You can mitigate the risk of system instability due to OOME by: performing tests to understand the memory requirements of your application before placing it into production, ensuring that your application runs only on hosts with adequate resources, limiting the amount of memory your container can use, being mindful when configuring swap on your Docker hosts as swap is slower than memory but can provide a buffer against running out of system memory, and considering converting your container to a service and using service-level constraints and node labels to ensure that the application runs only on hosts with enough memory. Docker can enforce hard or soft memory limits. Hard limits let the container use no more than a fixed amount of memory. Soft limits let the container use as much memory as it needs unless certain conditions are met, such as when the kernel detects low memory or contention on the host machine. Key memory options: -m or --memory sets the maximum amount of memory the container can use with a minimum allowed value of 6m (6 megabytes). --memory-swap is the amount of memory this container is allowed to swap to disk. --memory-swappiness allows setting a value between 0 and 100 to tune the percentage of anonymous pages the host kernel can swap out, by default the value is inherited from the host machine. --memory-reservation allows specifying a soft limit smaller than --memory which is activated when Docker detects contention or low memory on the host machine, it must be set lower than --memory for it to take precedence, because it is a soft limit it does not guarantee that the container does not exceed the limit. --kernel-memory sets the maximum amount of kernel memory the container can use with a minimum of 6m, because kernel memory cannot be swapped out a container which is starved of kernel memory may block host machine resources which can have side effects on the host machine and on other containers. --oom-kill-disable disables the OOM killer on a container but should only be used where you have also set the -m/--memory option, if the -m flag is not set the host can run out of memory and the kernel may need to kill the host system's processes to free memory. The --memory-swap flag only has meaning if --memory is also set. Using swap allows the container to write excess memory requirements to disk when the container has exhausted all the RAM available to it. If --memory-swap is set to a positive integer, then both --memory and --memory-swap must be set, --memory-swap represents the total amount of memory and swap that can be used and --memory controls the amount used by non-swap memory, so if --memory is 300m and --memory-swap is 1g the container can use 300m of memory and 700m swap. If --memory-swap is set to 0 the setting is ignored and the value is treated as unset. If --memory-swap is set to the same value as --memory and --memory is set to a positive integer, the container does not have access to swap. If --memory-swap is unset and --memory is set, the container can use as much swap as the --memory setting if the host container has swap memory configured, for instance if --memory is 300m and --memory-swap is not set the container can use 600m in total of memory and swap. If --memory-swap is explicitly set to -1, the container is allowed to use unlimited swap up to the amount available on the host system. Inside the container, tools like free report the host's available swap, not what is available inside the container. A --memory-swappiness value of 0 turns off anonymous page swapping, a value of 100 sets all anonymous pages as swappable. Kernel memory limits scenarios: unlimited memory with unlimited kernel memory is the default behavior. Unlimited memory with limited kernel memory is appropriate when the amount of memory needed by all cgroups is greater than the amount of memory that actually exists on the host machine. Limited memory with unlimited kernel memory means the overall memory is limited but the kernel memory is not. Limited memory with limited kernel memory is useful for debugging memory-related problems, if a container is using an unexpected amount of either type of memory it runs out of memory without affecting other containers or the host machine, within this setting if the kernel memory limit is lower than the user memory limit running out of kernel memory causes the container to experience an OOM error, if the kernel memory limit is higher than the user memory limit the kernel limit does not cause the container to experience an OOM. When you enable kernel memory limits, the host machine tracks high water mark statistics on a per-process basis so you can track which processes are using excess memory, this can be seen per process by viewing /proc/PID/status on the host machine. By default each container's access to the host machine's CPU cycles is unlimited. The CFS is the Linux kernel CPU scheduler for normal Linux processes. Key CPU options: --cpus specifies how much of the available CPU resources a container can use, for instance if the host machine has two CPUs and you set --cpus to 1.5 the container is guaranteed at most one and a half of the CPUs. --cpu-period specifies the CPU CFS scheduler period used alongside --cpu-quota, defaults to 100000 microseconds. --cpu-quota imposes a CPU CFS quota on the container, the number of microseconds per --cpu-period that the container is limited to before being throttled. --cpuset-cpus limits the specific CPUs or cores a container can use as a comma-separated list or hyphen-separated range. --cpu-shares set to a value greater or less than the default of 1024 to increase or reduce the container's weight and give it access to a greater or lesser proportion of the host machine's CPU cycles, this is only enforced when CPU cycles are constrained and is a soft limit.

title: Assign Memory Resources to Containers and Pods - Kubernetes Official Docs
raw_text: This page shows how to assign a memory request and a memory limit to a Container. A Container is guaranteed to have as much memory as it requests, but is not allowed to use more memory than its limit. To specify a memory request for a container, include the resources.requests.memory field in the container's resource manifest. To specify a memory limit, include resources.limits.memory. Example Pod configuration with one Container that has a memory request of 100 MiB and a memory limit of 200 MiB, using polinux/stress image with command stress --vm 1 --vm-bytes 150M --vm-hang 1. The args section provides arguments for the Container when it starts. The --vm-bytes 150M arguments tell the Container to attempt to allocate 150 MiB of memory. Create the Pod with kubectl apply, verify the Pod Container is running with kubectl get pod, and view detailed information with kubectl get pod --output=yaml showing resources requests memory 100Mi and limits memory 200Mi. Run kubectl top pod to fetch the metrics for the pod. The output shows that the Pod is using about 162,900,000 bytes of memory, which is about 150 MiB. This is greater than the Pod's 100 MiB request, but within the Pod's 200 MiB limit. A Container can exceed its memory request if the Node has memory available. But a Container is not allowed to use more than its memory limit. If a Container allocates more memory than its limit, the Container becomes a candidate for termination. If the Container continues to consume memory beyond its limit, the Container is terminated. If a terminated Container can be restarted, the kubelet restarts it, as with any other type of runtime failure. Example Pod configuration with one Container that has a memory request of 50 MiB and a memory limit of 100 MiB, where the Container will attempt to allocate 250 MiB of memory which is well above the 100 MiB limit. At this point, the Container might be running or killed. Repeat the command until the Container is killed, showing NAME memory-demo-2 READY 0/1 STATUS OOMKilled RESTARTS 1 AGE 24s. Get a more detailed view of the Container status with kubectl get pod --output=yaml showing lastState terminated exitCode 137 reason OOMKilled. The Container in this exercise can be restarted, so the kubelet restarts it. Repeat the command several times to see that the Container is repeatedly killed and restarted: OOMKilled, then Running, then OOMKilled again. View detailed information about the Pod history with kubectl describe pod showing Normal Created and Warning BackOff Back-off restarting failed container. View detailed information about cluster Nodes with kubectl describe nodes, the output includes a record of the Container being killed because of an out-of-memory condition: Warning OOMKilling Memory cgroup out of memory: Kill process 4481 (stress) score 1994 or sacrifice child. Memory requests and limits are associated with Containers, but it is useful to think of a Pod as having a memory request and limit. The memory request for the Pod is the sum of the memory requests for all the Containers in the Pod. Likewise, the memory limit for the Pod is the sum of the limits of all the Containers in the Pod.
